perturbation.original_text: Vendor shall notify AT&T and provide AT&T with all necessary information (including but not limited OSHA Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)) at least thirty (30) days before shipping Material containing or consisting of Regulated Substances to AT&T or commencing the performance of Services for AT&T involving the handling or use of Regulated Substances. Each MSDS must include information indicating the specific worker protection equipment requirement for use with the Regulated Substance covered thereby.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor shall notify AT&T if shipping Material containing or consisting of Regulated Substances to AT&T or commencing the performance of Services for AT&T involving the handling or use of Regulated Substances. Each MSDS may include information indicating the specific worker protection equipment requirement for use with the Regulated Substance covered thereby.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'provide AT&T with all necessary information (including but not limited OSHA Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)) at least thirty (30) days' and changing 'must' to 'may', the contract now fails to mandate the provision of MSDS sheets, potentially violating OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard and related state laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard mandates that employers receive and maintain Safety Data Sheets (SDS, formerly MSDS) for hazardous chemicals in the workplace. By removing the requirement for Vendor to provide these, AT&T is put at risk of non-compliance with OSHA regulations, as they may not have the necessary safety information readily available.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication. Part Number: 1910 Part Number Title: Occupational Safety and Health Standards Standard Number: 1910.1200 Title: Hazard Communication. Appendix: A B C D E F GPO Source: e-CFR 1910.1200(a) Purpose . 1910.1200(a)(1) The purpose of this section is to ensure that the hazards of all chemicals produced or imported are classified, and that information concerning the classified hazards is transmitted to employers and employees. The requirements of this section are intended to be consistent with the provisions of the United Nations Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals (GHS), primarily Revision 7. The transmittal of information is to be accomplished by means of comprehensive hazard communication programs, which are to include container labeling and other forms of warning, safety data sheets and employee training. 1910.1200(a)(2) This occupational safety and health standard is intended to address comprehensively the issue of classifying the potential hazards of chemicals, and communicating information concerning hazards and appropriate protective measures to employees, and to preempt any legislative or regulatory enactments of a state, or political subdivision of a state, pertaining to this subject. Classifying the potential hazards of chemicals and communicating information concerning hazards and appropriate protective measures to employees, may include, for example, but is not limited to, provisions for: developing and maintaining a written hazard communication program for the workplace, including lists of hazardous chemicals present; labeling of containers of chemicals in the workplace, as well as of containers of chemicals being shipped to other workplaces; preparation and distribution of safety data sheets to employees and downstream employers; and development and implementation of employee

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard. The original contract mandates that the vendor provide AT&T with all necessary information, including MSDS sheets, at least 30 days before shipping regulated substances. The changed text removes this mandatory provision. Law Snippet 1 states that 'the transmittal of information is to be accomplished by means of comprehensive hazard communication programs, which are to include container labeling and other forms of warning, safety data sheets and employee training.' By making the provision of MSDS sheets optional ('may include'), the changed text weakens AT&T's ability to comply with OSHA's requirement for comprehensive hazard communication, creating a legal contradiction by omission.

perturbation.original_text: To the fullest extent allowable by Law, the policy must include a waiver of subrogation in favor of AT&T, its Affiliates, and their directors, officers and employees.
perturbation.changed_text: The policy may include a waiver of subrogation in favor of AT&T, its Affiliates, and their directors, officers and employees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must' to 'may' removes the mandatory requirement for a waiver of subrogation, increasing AT&T's risk.  Without a waiver, the insurance company could subrogate against AT&T, potentially leading to litigation and financial losses for AT&T, violating standard risk mitigation practices required under state business laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Insurance Law - Subrogation
perturbation.law_explanation: Insurance law often considers subrogation rights unless explicitly waived. By making the waiver optional, the contract fails to ensure AT&T is protected from potential claims by Vendor's insurers. This contradicts the implicit requirement in business law to act in good faith and mitigate foreseeable risks through clear contractual terms, leading to potential legal disputes and financial liabilities for AT&T.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - Unofficial New York Codes, Rules and Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'must' to 'may' directly contradicts the original contract's intent to mandate a waiver of subrogation, which is a risk mitigation measure. The Perturbation Explanation states that this removal increases AT&T's risk, which could lead to financial losses. The Law Explanation supports that mitigating foreseeable risks through clear contractual terms is a sound business practice. This falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition because it removes an essential requirement that served as a consumer (AT&T's) protection. Although Scraped Snippets are not useful, the change undermines a risk mitigation measure. Since the original text mandated action, and now it's optional, it weakens the contract and creates a situation where AT&T is less protected, which they should be.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor will provide, at no additional cost to AT&T, full and complete technical assistance to AT&T for the Material and Services provisioned under this Agreement, including ongoing technical support and field service and assistance, and telephone assistance to assist with installation, operation, maintenance and problem resolution as set forth in the terms of the Maintenance Addendum.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor will use reasonable efforts to provide technical assistance to AT&T for the Material and Services provisioned under this Agreement, including ongoing technical support and field service and assistance, and telephone assistance to assist with installation, operation, maintenance and problem resolution as set forth in the terms of the Maintenance Addendum.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'full and complete' to 'reasonable efforts' significantly weakens the obligation. This could violate consumer protection laws relating to warranties and service contracts, as it provides a loophole for the vendor to not fully support the services being provided. Many jurisdictions, such as California, have consumer protection laws related to reasonable efforts in service provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) implies a duty to provide reasonable care and skill in rendering services. By changing the obligation to 'reasonable efforts', the contract could be seen as attempting to limit liability and diminish the expected standard of care in providing technical assistance, potentially violating the CLRA's prohibition against deceptive and unconscionable acts in consumer transactions. This is particularly problematic when technical support is critical to the functioning of the services being provided.

CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.) 1751. Any waiver by a consumer of the provisions of this title is contrary to public policy and shall be unenforceable and void. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.) 1752. The provisions of this title are not exclusive. The remedies provided herein for violation of any section of this title or for conduct proscribed by any section of this title shall be in addition to any other procedures or remedies for any violation or conduct provided for in any other law. Nothing in this title shall limit any other statutory or any common law rights of the Attorney General or any other person to bring class actions. Class actions by consumers brought under the specific provisions of Chapter 3 (commencing with Section 1770) of this title shall be governed exclusively by the provisions of Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 1780); however, this shall not be construed so as to deprive a consumer of any statutory or common law right to bring a class action without resort to this title. If any act or practice proscribed under this title also constitutes a cause of action in common law or a violation of another statute, the consumer may assert such common law or statutory cause of action under the procedures and with the remedies provided for in such law. (Amended by Stats. 1975, Ch. 615.) 1753. If any provision of this title or the application thereof to any person or circumstance is held to be unconstitutional, the remainder of the title and the application of such provision to other persons or circumstances shall not be affected thereby. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.) 1754. The provisions of this title shall not apply to any transaction which provides for the construction, sale, or construction and sale of an entire residence or all or part
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'full and complete' technical assistance, while the changed version requires only 'reasonable efforts'. This shift weakens the vendor's obligation, potentially conflicting with consumer protection laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the California Consumers Legal Remedies Act (CLRA), states that waivers of consumer rights are 'unenforceable and void.' Requiring only 'reasonable efforts' could be interpreted as an attempt to waive the consumer's right to full and complete support, creating a contradiction. The change undermines the legally expected standard of care, making the agreement potentially non-compliant.
